question:
In which of the following conversion, bond length increases?
(P) $\mathrm{NO} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}^{+}$
(Q) $\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}$
(R) $\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$
(S) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}$
explanation:
Bond length $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { Bond order }}$,
if BO same, BL depends on no. of antibonding electrons
(P) $\mathrm{NO} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}^{+}$(B. O. increases 2.5 to 3 ).
(Q) ${\rm{N}}_2^ + \to {\rm{N}}_2^ - $ [B.O. is same (2.5) but no. of

(R) $\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$(Bond order increase, 2 to 2.5)
(S) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}^{+}$(Bond order decrease, 1 to 0.5 )

question:

Reason: Lone pair - lone pair repulsion is stronger in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ than in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953137/preptracker/questions/q_16343_explanation_1764953137435.png Oxygen is more electronegative than sulphur due to which there is less lone pair - lone pair repulsion in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ while more lone pair - lone pair repulsion in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$. Hence both Assertion  and  Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

question: The decreasing values of bond angles from ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\left( {{\rm{106^\circ }}} \right)\,\,{\rm{to}}\,\,{\rm{Sb}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\left( {{\rm{101^\circ }}} \right)$ down group-15 of the periodic table is due to
explanation:
The bond angle decreases on moving down the group due to decrease in bond pair-bond pair repulsion.
$\mathop {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}\limits_{{\rm{107^\circ }}} \,\,\,\mathop {{\rm{P}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}\limits_{{\rm{94^\circ }}} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{As}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}\limits_{{\rm{92^\circ }}} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Sb}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}\limits_{{\rm{91^\circ }}} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Bi}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}\limits_{{\rm{90^\circ }}} $
This can also be explained by the fact that as the size of central atom increases and its electronegativity decreases. Thus distance between bond pairs of electron increases and bp-bp repulsion decreases. As a result bond angle decreases from ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}\,\,{\rm{to}}\,\,{\rm{Bi}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{.}}$

question: For a stable molecule the value of bond order must be
explanation:
For a stable molecule the value of bond order nust be positive as bond order.
No. of bonding electrons – No. of antibonding
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{electron}}}}{{\rm{2}}}$
${\rm{Bond}}\,\,{\rm{order}}\,\,\left( {{\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}} \right){\rm{ = }}\frac{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ - }}{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{a}}}}}{{\rm{2}}}$
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{b}}}{\rm{ = }}$ No. of electron in bonding M.O'S
${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{a}}}{\rm{ = }}$ No. of electron in anti bonding ${\rm{M}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{s}}}$
The electrons in the bonding molecular orbital leads to lowering in energy and hence higher in stability whereas in antibonding molecular orbital leads to destability.
(i) $\left( {{\rm{ - ve}}} \right)$ bond order $ \Rightarrow $ greater number of electrons in antibonding molecular orbital than bonding molecular orbital. Therefore molecule is less stable compared to the separated atoms.
(ii) 0 bond order $ \Rightarrow $ number of i electrons in bonding molecular orbital = number of electrons in antibonding molecular orbital. Therefore there is no gain in energy in forming the molecule i.e. molecules does not exists
(iii) $\left( {{\rm{ + ve}}} \right)$ bond order $ \Rightarrow $ number of electrons in bonding molecular orbital is more than those in no of electrons antibonding molecular orbital. Therefore there is a gain in energy while the bonding takes place.

options.text: there is no relationship between stability and bond order

question:
Bond length depends upon the size of atoms, hybridisation, resonance, bond order etc. In the following relations,
I. Bond length $ \propto $ size of atoms
II. Bond length $ \propto $ hybridisation (no. of hybrid orbitals)
III. Bond length $ \propto $ bond order
Choose the correct option.
explanation:
Statements I and II are correct while the statement III is incorrect. It’s correct form is as follows:
Bond length is inversely proportional to bond order.
${\rm{Bond}}\,\,{\rm{length}} \propto \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{bond}}\,\,{\rm{order}}}}$
So, the option (1) is correct.

question: What is the nature of the bonding in anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and hydrated $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ respectively?
explanation: In anhydrous condition, the (charge/radius) ratio, i.e., polarisability of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ is high and hence, according to Fajans' rule, $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ polarises $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ions to large extent, there introducing covalent character in the compound, i.e., $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ behaves as a covalent compound in anhydrous conditions. In aqueous medium the ions get hydrated, because the amount of hydration enthalpy released exceeds, the sum total of ionisation enthalpy required.Since, the (charge/radius) ratio of hydrated aluminium ion is much smaller as compared to that of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$, the tendency of $\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ to polarise hydrated $\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}$ ion decreases and the resulting hydrated compound is ionic in nature.

question: What is the bonding nature in LiCl bond?
explanation:
LiCl is ionic because Li is metal and Cl is non-metal and has electrostatic force of attraction between positive $\left(\mathrm{Li}^{+}\right)$and negative $\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)$ion.
LiCl is predominantly covalent because smaller size of $\mathrm{Li}^{\oplus}$ easily polarise the larger size of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ion. Thus, it is covalent as well as ionic.

question: Identify the set of molecules which are not in the correct order of their dipole moments.

Dipole moment $(\mu)=\mathrm{q} \times \mathrm{d}$
$\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{HF}-1.820 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=1.5 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{NH}_{3}-1.4 \mathrm{D} \\\mathrm{HCl}-1.080 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-0.95 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{NF}_{3}-0.24 \mathrm{D} \\\mathrm{HBr}-0.827 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{CO}_{2}-0.00 \mathrm{D} & \mathrm{BF}_{3}-0.00 \mathrm{D}\end{array}$
The correct order of dipole moment is $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} < \mathrm{HCl} < \mathrm{HF} .$

question: Identify the option in which the molecules are arranged in the correct order of their dipole moments
explanation: The correct order of dipole moment is $\mathrm{BF}_{3} < \mathrm{NF}_{3} < \mathrm{NH}_{3} . \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ have pyramidal geometry where central nitrogen has 3 bond pairs and 1 lone pair. Hence, both are $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$-hybridised. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, dipole vector of bond and lone pair are in same direction while in $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$, they are in opposite direction. Hence, dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is larger than $\mathrm{NF}_{3} . \mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is a symmetrical molecule where dipole vectors are arranged at $120^{\circ}$. Therefore, the resultant of two dipole vectors cancel out with the third dipole vector. Hence it's dipole moment will be zero.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the nature of the bonding in anhydrous $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ and hydrated $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ respectively?
explanation: In anhydrous condition, the (charge/radius) ratio, i.e., polarisability of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ is high and hence, according to Fajans' rule, $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ polarises $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ions to large extent, there introducing covalent character in the compound, i.e., $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ behaves as a covalent compound in anhydrous conditions. In aqueous medium the ions get hydrated, because the amount of hydration enthalpy released exceeds, the sum total of ionisation enthalpy required.Since, the (charge/radius) ratio of hydrated aluminium ion is much smaller as compared to that of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$, the tendency of $\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ to polarise hydrated $\mathrm{Cl}^{\ominus}$ ion decreases and the resulting hydrated compound is ionic in nature.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the bonding nature in LiCl bond?
explanation:
LiCl is ionic because Li is metal and Cl is non-metal and has electrostatic force of attraction between positive $\left(\mathrm{Li}^{+}\right)$and negative $\left(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right)$ion.
LiCl is predominantly covalent because smaller size of $\mathrm{Li}^{\oplus}$ easily polarise the larger size of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ion. Thus, it is covalent as well as ionic.

question: Which is the correct order of dipole moments of $\mathrm{BF}_{3}, \mathrm{NF}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ has zero dipole moment. In $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{~N}$ is more electronegative than H . So, nitrogen pulls the electron from H towards itself and the direction of moment is same due to $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}$ bond as that of the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957847/preptracker/questions/q_103977_explanation_1764957847360.png
In $\mathrm{NF}_{3}, \mathrm{~F}$ is more electronegative than N. All F atoms pull the electrons towards themselves and resultant dipole moment is opposite to the direction of that of the lone pair at N atom. Hence, net dipole moment decreases.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957848/preptracker/questions/q_103977_explanation_1764957848190.png
Therefore, dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is greater than $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$. Hence, order of dipole moment is$\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{NF}_{3}>\mathrm{BF}_{3}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The covalency of Al in $\left[\mathrm{AlCl}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5}\right]^{2+}$ is
explanation: Covalency is the number of bonds formed by central metal atom with ligands. In$\left[\mathrm{AlCl}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5}\right]^{+2}, \mathrm{Al}$ central metal atom is bonded by coordination bonds with one chloride and 5 water molecules. So, covalency is 6 .

question:
Identify the correct statements from the following.
(i) The dipole moment of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is zero
(ii) The dipole moment of $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ is higher than the dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
(iii) The dipole moment of HI is lower than the dipole moment of HCl .
explanation:
i. The $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ molecule has a symmetrical trigonal planar geometry, the moment of any two $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{F}$ dipoles is equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the moment of the $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ molecule is zero, same as $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
ii. In $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{~N}$ is more electronegative than H so, N pulls the electrons from H towards itself so, the direction of moment due to the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds is in the same direction as that of the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951330/preptracker/questions/q_103965_explanation_1764951330033.png
But in case of N - lone pair moment is in the opposite direction to the N-F bond moments. Thus, $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ has smaller net dipole moment.
iii. Electronegativity of I-atom is less than of Cl -atom. Hence, HI has lower dipole-moment than HCl .
subject: Chemistry

Reason: In ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ ,the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the N-H bonds.F is the most electronegative element.
explanation:
Both the molecules ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ have pyramidal shape with a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom.
Although fluorine is more electronegative than nitrogen, the resultant dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}\left(4.90 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}\right)}}$ is greater than that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}\left(0.8 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}\right)}}$. This is because, in case of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}}$ bonds whereas in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ the orbital dipole is in the direction opposite to the resultant dipole moment of the three N-F bonds. The orbital dipole because of lone pair decreases the effect of the resultant bond moment which results in low dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ as represented below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959067/preptracker/questions/q_17381_explanation_1764959066614.png So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: group moment is $2.3 \,\mathrm{D}$ and $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group moment is $0.4 \,\mathrm{D}$. Determine the dipole moment of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$ in $\mathrm{cm} \times 10^{30}$ in nearest possible integer.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959057/preptracker/questions/q_17274_explanation_1764959056904.png
The resultant dipole moment of two $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}$ bonds.
$=\sqrt{(0.4)^{2}+(0.4)^{2}+2 \times(0.4)^{2} \times \cos 120^{\circ}}$
$ = \sqrt {0.32 - 0.16} = 0.4\,\,{\rm{D}}$
$\therefore$ Net dipole moment of the molecule $ = 2.3 + 0.4 = 2.7\,\,{\rm{D}}$
$1 \mathrm{D}=10^{-18}$ esu $-\mathrm{cm} .=10^{-20}$ esu-m.
$4.8 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,\,{\rm{esu}} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}{\rm{C}}$
$\therefore 1$ esu $=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$.
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{D}=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 10^{-29} \mathrm{cm}=\dfrac{10}{3} \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}$.
$\therefore$ Net dipole moment of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$
$=2.7 \times \dfrac{10}{3} \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}=9 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}$

question: A diatomic molecule has a dipole moment of 1.2 D. If its bond distance is ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{A^\circ }}$, what fraction of an electronic charge exists on each atom?
explanation:
For a diatomic molecule dipole moment ${\rm{\mu = e \times d}}$
$\therefore $ electronic charge ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{\mu }}}{{\rm{d}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 18}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}\,\,{\rm{cm}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 8}}}}\,\,{\rm{cm}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}$
Actual value of electronic charge
${\rm{ = 4}}{\rm{.8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}{\rm{.}}$
$\therefore $ Fraction of the electronic charge
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}}}{{{\rm{4}}{\rm{.8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.25}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: The formal charge on the central oxygen atom in ozone is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956683/preptracker/questions/q_104263_explanation_1764956682998.png
Formal charge $=$ Total number of valence electrons in the free atom - Total number of nonbonding electron (lone pair) $-\dfrac{1}{2}$ [Total number of bonding electrons] (shared electron).
Formal charge on O - atom numbered 1
$ = 6 - 4 - \frac{4}{2} = 0$
Formal charge on O - atom numbered 2$=6-2-\dfrac{6}{2}=+1$
Formal charge on O-atom numbered 3$=6-6-\dfrac{2}{2}=-1$
subject: Chemistry

question:
The incomplete Lewis representation of $\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}$ is given below. The formal charge on atoms marked as $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c respectively, are
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957870/preptracker/questions/q_104018_question_1764957869526.png
explanation:
Correct Lewis representation is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957870/preptracker/questions/q_104018_explanation_1764957870405.png
Formal charge $=$ Number of valence electrons – number of lone pair electrons $-\dfrac{1}{2} \times$ number of bond pair electrons
So, formal charge on
$\mathrm{a}=4-0-\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 8\right)=4-4=0$
$b=6-4-\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4\right)=6-6=0$
$c=6-6-\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2\right)=6-7=-1$
Hence, a : 0, b: 0, c : – 1 .
subject: Chemistry

The formal charges on atom 1, 2 and 3 are +1, 0 and -1 respectively

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Molecules with only s and p orbitals cannot exhibit hybridization.

explanation: Statement  A  is false because hybridization often involves only  s  and  p  orbitals, especially in lighter elements like carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Statement B is true, noting that while hybridization frequently involves s and p orbitals, d orbitals can also participate, particularly in transition metals. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The number of hybrid orbitals formed is always equal to the number of atomic orbitals mixed in the hybridization process.
Hybrid orbitals are formed to maximize the electron pair repulsion, resulting in geometric arrangements that minimize energy.
explanation: Statement A is true because the process of hybridization conserves the number of orbitals; it rearranges the existing orbitals into new, equivalent forms. Statement B is also true, explaining that the formation of hybrid orbitals is driven by the need to minimize electron repulsion, which aligns with the VSEPR theory. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.

question: The hybridisation and shape of $\mathrm{I}_{3}^{-}$ion, respectively, are
explanation:
Hybridisation $=\dfrac{1}{2}$ (Number of valence electron + number of monovalent atoms other than central atom + negative charge - positive charge)
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(7+2+1)=\dfrac{10}{2}$
$=5 \cong \mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951790/preptracker/questions/q_104013_explanation_1764951790466.png

question:
Find out the hybridisations of $\mathrm{C}_{1}, \mathrm{C}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{3}$ labelled carbon atoms in the given molecule
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955391/preptracker/questions/q_104002_question_1764955391229.png
explanation:
Among hydrocarbon bonds, the carbon atoms, which do not contain any pi $(\pi)$ bond show $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridisation. The carbon atoms, that contain one pi $(\pi)$ bond show $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ and which contain two pi $(\pi)$ bonds show sp hybridisation, because pi $(\pi)$ bonds do not get hybridised.
Thus, $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)=\mathrm{sp}$-hybridisation
$\left( {{{\text{C}}_2}} \right) = {\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^2}{\text{ - }}\,{\text{hybridisation }}$
$\left( {{{\text{C}}_3}} \right) = {\text{sp}}\,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{hybridisation}}$
Hence, option (2) is correct.

explanation:
${\rm{In}}\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{,}}\,{\rm{No}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\rm{of}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\sigma }} - {\rm{bonds}} = 3$

Total no. of electron pairs $ = 3 + 1 = 4\,;\,{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^3}$ hybridisation.

${\text{H}} = \frac{1}{2}({\text{V}} + {\text{M}} - {\text{C}} + {\text{A}})$

question: The structure of  $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$  ion is
explanation:
${\rm{H}} = \frac{1}{2}({\rm{V}} + {\rm{M}} - {\rm{C}} + {\rm{A}})$
where H = No of orbitals involved in hybridisation
V = No.of valance electrons of central atom
M = No. of monovalent atoms linked with central atom
C = Charge on the cation
A = Charge on the anion
${\rm{H}} = \frac{1}{2}[5 + 0 - 0 + 1] = 3,{\rm{s}}{{\rm{p}}^2}$ hybridisation
So, structure is triangular planar.

question:
Assertion: The hybridisation possessed by boron atom in $\mathrm{BCl}_{3}$ molecule is $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$.

Reason: $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridised molecules have trigonal planar geometry.
explanation:
Hybridisation $=\dfrac{1}{2}(\mathrm{VE}+\mathrm{M}-$ charge $)$
$\left(\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{M}=\text { no.of monovalent atoms } \\ \text { attached } \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{H}\end{array}\right)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2}(3+3-0)=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^{2}$
$\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybrid orbitals arrange in trigonal planar manner. Thus the option (2) is correct.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: In $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$, molecule, $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}$ bond angle is $90^{\circ}$.


Reason: $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$ molecule has octahedral geometry with $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ hybridisation.
explanation: Formation of $\mathrm{SF}_{6}: \mathrm{InSF}_{6}$, the central sulphur atom has the ground state outer electronic configuration  ${\text{3}}{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{ 3}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{4}}}$  In the excited state, the available six orbitals i.e. one $\mathrm{s}$, three $\mathrm{p}$ and two $\mathrm{d}$ are singly occupied by electrons. These orbitals hybridise to form six new $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ hybrid orbitals which are projected towards the six corners of a regular octahedron in $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$. In this, $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{F}$ bond angle are $90^{\circ}$ and $180^{\circ}$.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect

question: Match the following columns based on the concept of hydrogen bonding and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
(A - R) Hydrogen bond is the attractive force that binds a hydrogen atom of one molecule with the electronegative atom of another molecule.
(B - P) The highly electronegative elements that participate in hydrogen bonding are Nitrogen, Oxygen, and Fluorine.
(C - S) Intermolecular hydrogen bond is formed between hydrogen atom of one molecule and electronegative atom of a different molecule.
(D - Q) Intramolecular hydrogen bond is a bond formed when a hydrogen atom is within the same molecule between two highly electronegative atoms. Correct option is (3).

options.text: A - Q,    B - P, 	C - S, 	D - R
A - R,    B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - R,    B - P, 	C - S, 	D - Q
A - S,    B - R, 	C - Q, 	D - P

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Water has a high boiling point due to hydrogen bonding.
Hydrogen bonding only occurs in polar solvents.
explanation: Statement A is true because the extensive hydrogen bonding in water contributes to its relatively high boiling point. Statement B is false because while hydrogen bonding is prominent in polar solvents, it is not exclusive to them; hydrogen bonds can form in any situation where a hydrogen is bound to a highly electronegative atom and is in proximity to a lone pair of electrons, even in less polar environments. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

explanation: Statement  A  is false because hydrogen bonds are typically weaker than covalent bonds. Statement B is true as hydrogen bonds are critical in maintaining the structure and function of biological macromolecules. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

Hydrogen bonds can form between molecules where hydrogen is covalently bonded to a strongly electronegative atom like nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine.

explanation: Statement  A  is true as hydrogen bonds commonly occur in molecules where hydrogen is bonded to nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine. Statement B is false because hydrogen bonds are a type of strong dipole-dipole attractions, not ionic bonds. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Assertion: The nearly tetrahedral arrangement of the orbitals about the oxygen atom allows each water molecule to form hydrogen bonds with as many as four neighbouring water molecules.

Reason: In ice, each molecule forms four hydrogen bonds as each molecule is fixed in the space.
explanation: The near-tetrahedral arrangement of orbitals around the oxygen atom in water enables the formation of hydrogen bonds with multiple neighboring water molecules, as the electrostatic attraction between partial charges on oxygen and hydrogen atoms allows each water molecule to engage in up to four hydrogen bonds in various directions, a feature also present in ice. Thus the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is liquid while $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is a gas.

Reason: H-bonding is present in both the cases.
explanation: Hydrogen bonding is observed in water due to the attraction between a partially positive hydrogen atom and a highly electronegative oxygen atom. This contributes to water's liquid state. question: Which of the following substance has the highest melting point?
explanation: Melting point depends upon lattice energy. $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{KCl}$ have unit charge on their ions while $\mathrm{MgO}$ and $\mathrm{BaO}$ have two units of charge, therefore lattice energies of $\mathrm{MgO}$ and $\mathrm{BaO}$ are expected to be larger than those of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ and $\mathrm{KCl}$ Since, $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ is smaller than $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$, therefore, $\mathrm{MgO}$ has the higher lattice energy and hence $\mathrm{MgO}$ has the highest melting point.
subject: Chemistry

question: The higher lattice energy corresponds to
explanation:
Lattice energy $\propto$ charge of ion
$\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { size of ion }}$
On moving downward, the size of alkaline earth metals increases due to increase in number of shells. Thus, Mg has the smallest size.
Hence, lattice energy is higher for $\mathrm{MgO}$.

As the distance increases, the electrostatic attraction weakens, causing a rapid decrease in lattice enthalpy. So the option (1) is correct.

question: An atom of element A has three electrons in its outer shell and B has six electrons in its outer most shell. The formula of the compound formed between these will be
explanation: In this case the valence electron in the atom A is three and hence its valency is generally 3. In the atom B, the number of valence electron is six. Hence, its valency is usually 2. An example of two such elements are Al and O and the formula of Aluminium oxide is ${\rm{A}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}$.

C. $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}>\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{O}-$ bond length
D. $\mathrm{N}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{H}_{2}-$ bond enthalpy

$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\rm{\mu }}({\rm{D}})}\\ {{\rm{A}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}}&{1.85}\\ {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}}&{1.47}\\ {{\rm{CHC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}}&{1.04} \end{array}$

$\mathrm{XeO}_{3}: 1$ lone pair of electron
$\mathrm{XeF}_{2}: 3$ lone pairs of electron

D. $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ : Bond order is 3
$\mathrm{H}_{2}$ : Bond order is 1
$\mathrm{O}_{2}$ : Bond order is 2
Bond order $\propto$ Bond enthalpy
Order of bond enthalpy: $\mathrm{N}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{H}_{2}$

question: Using MOT, compare $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$and $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$species and choose the incorrect option:
explanation:
Molecular orbital configuration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$:$\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^{2}=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^{1}$
Bond order $=\dfrac{1}{2}(10-5)=2.5$
Number of unpaired electrons $=1$, hence paramagnetic.
Molecular orbital configuration of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$, hence
$\sigma 1\,{{\text{s}}^2}{\sigma ^*}1\,{{\text{s}}^2}\sigma 2\,{{\text{s}}^2}{\sigma ^*}2\,{{\text{s}}^2}\sigma 2{\text{p}}_{\text{z}}^2\,\pi 2{\text{p}}_{\text{x}}^2$ $ = \pi 2{\text{p}}_{\text{y}}^2\,{\pi ^*}2{\text{p}}_{\text{x}}^2 = {\pi ^*}2{\text{p}}_{\text{y}}^1$
Bond order $=\dfrac{1}{2}(10-7)=1.5$
Number of unpaired electron $=1$, hence paramagnetic. $\,\,$ Both $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$ are paramagnetic. $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}$ have higher bond order than $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$, hence $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}$ is less stable.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Electronic Configuration and Molecular Behaviour Different Molecules

question: Identify the pair in which difference in bond order value is maximum
explanation:
Bond order $=\dfrac{1}{2}$ [ Number of bonding electrons - Number of anti bonding electrons]
The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}[10-6]=2$
The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}=\dfrac{1}{2}[10-7]=1.5$
The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}=\dfrac{1}{2}[10-5]=2.5$
The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2+}=\dfrac{1}{2}[10-8]=1$
The bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2+}=\dfrac{1}{2}[10-4]=3$ The difference between $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2+}$ is
$(3-1)=2($ maximum $)$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Electronic Configuration and Molecular Behaviour Different Molecules

question: According to molecular orbital theory, which of the following statement is not correct?
explanation:
$\mathrm{C}_{2}=12$ electrons
Electronic configuration of $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ molecule is, $(\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s})^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~s}\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 \mathrm{~s})^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~s}\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2}=\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^{2}$
Bond order $=\dfrac{1}{2}(8-4)=\dfrac{4}{2}=2$.
So, $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ is diamagnetic and bond order is '2'.
$\mathrm{C}_{2}^{-}$ is paramagnetic in nature.
$\mathrm{C}_{2}$ consists of both pi bonds because of the presence of four electrons in two pi molecular orbitals.
Hence, statement (4) is not correct.

explanation: The filling of molecular orbitals is indeed guided by the same principles that apply to atomic orbitals. question: The correct sequence of bond order is
explanation:
Bond order $({\text{BO}}) = \frac{{{{\text{N}}_{\text{b}}} - {{\text{N}}_{\text{a}}}}}{2}$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+} ;$B.O. $=\dfrac{10-5}{2}=2.5$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-} ;$B.O. $=\dfrac{10-7}{2}=1.5$
$\mathrm{O}_{2} ;$ B.O. $=\dfrac{10-6}{2}=2$
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-2} ;$ B.O. $=\dfrac{10-8}{2}=1$
$\therefore$ Correct order of bond order is
$\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}>\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$.

question:
The linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals takes place only when the combining atomic orbitals
(I) have the same energy
(II) have the minimum overlap
(III) have same symmetry about the molecular axis
(IV) have different symmetry about the molecular axis
Choose the most appropriate from the options given below :
explanation:
Conditions for the combination of atomic orbitals are as follows :
(I) The combining atomic orbitals must have same or nearly same energy.
(II) The combining atomic orbitals must have the same symmetry about the molecular axis.
(III) The combining atomic orbitals must overlap to the maximum extent.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement ${\mathrm{A}}$ and Statement B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
A ${\mathrm{\pi}}$ bonding MO has lower electron density above and below the internuclear axis.
The ${{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}$ anti-bonding MO has a node between the nuclei.
explanation:
Molecular orbitals obtained from ${\mathrm{2 p_{x}}}$ and ${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}}}$ orbitals are not symmetrical around the bond axis because of the presence of positive lobes above and negative lobes below the molecular plane. Such molecular orbitals, are labelled as ${\mathrm{\pi}}$ and ${\mathrm{\pi^{*}}}$. A ${\mathrm{\pi}}$ bonding MO has larger electron density above and below the internuclear axis. The antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.
So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: When ${\mathrm{\Psi_{\mathrm{A}}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\Psi_{\mathrm{B}}}}$ are the wave functions of atomic orbitals, then ${\mathrm{\sigma^{*}}}$ is represented by
explanation:

So, the correct option is (1).

Bond order $=\dfrac{4-3}{2}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5$$\therefore y=0.5$$\therefore(x-y)=(1-0.5)=0.5$

question:
Assertion: Fluorine molecule has bond order one.

Reason: The number of electrons in the antibonding molecular orbitals is two less than that in bonding molecular orbitals.
explanation:
The electronic configuration of the ${{\text{F}}_{\text{2}}}$ molecule can be represented as follows:
$\mathrm{F}_{2}: \sigma 1 \mathrm{~S}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~S}^{2}, \sigma 2 \mathrm{~S}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~S}^{2}, \sigma 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{z}}{ }^{2}$, $\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{x}}{ }^{2}=\pi 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{y}}{ }^{2}\right), \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{P}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{y}}{ }^{2}$
Bond Order $(\mathrm{BO})=($ No. of electrons in bonding MOs - No. of electrons in antibonding MOs) / 2
No. of electrons in bonding MOs $=10$ electrons No. of electrons in antibonding MOs $=8$ electrons
$\mathrm{BO}=(10-8) / 2=2 / 2=1$
Thus, the bond order of the $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ molecule is 1. So the option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: Using MOT, compare ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ species and choose the incorrect option:
explanation:
Molecular orbital configuration of ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}\,{\rm{:}}$
${\rm{\sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\sigma 2p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}{\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi 2p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{1}}$
Bond order ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 5}}} \right){\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.5}}$
Number of unpaired electrons = 1, hence paramagnetic.
Molecular orbital configuration of ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{,}}$ hence
${\rm{\sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\sigma 2p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi 2p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{ = }}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{\pi 2p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{2}}{\mkern 1mu} {{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}$
${\rm{ = }}{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{1}}$
Bond order ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 7}}} \right){\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.5}}$
Number of unpaired electron = 1, hence paramagnetic.
Both ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ are paramagnetic.
${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$ have higher bond order than ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$, hence ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ is less stable.

question: The increasing order of bond order ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}{\rm{,}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{ - 2}}}\,\,{\rm{is}}$
explanation:
Molecular orbital electronic configuration of ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is
${\left( {{\rm{\sigma 1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{z}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}$
${\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{1}}}{\rm{ = }}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{1}}}$
$\therefore \,{\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 6}}} \right){\rm{ = 2}}$
Molecular orbital electronic configuration of ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$
${\left( {{\rm{\sigma 1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{z}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}$
${\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{1}}}$
$\therefore \,{\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 5}}} \right){\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.5}}$
Molecular orbital electronic configuration of ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$
is : ${\left( {{\rm{\sigma 1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{z}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}$
${\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = }}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{1}}}$
$\therefore \,{\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 7}}} \right){\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.5}}$
Molecular orbital electronic configuration of ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}$ is :
${\left( {{\rm{\sigma 1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2s}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{z}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}$
${\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{\rm{\pi 2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{x}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}{\left( {{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}_{\rm{y}}}} \right)^{\rm{2}}}$
$\therefore \,{\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}\,\,{\rm{of}}\,\,{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{10 - 8}}} \right){\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.0}}$
$\therefore $ Increasing order of B.O. is: ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ < O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ < }}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ < O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$

explanation:

question: What is the bond order of ${{\rm{B}}_{\rm{2}}}$ molecule?

Bond order ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}$ (Number of bonding electrons - number of anti-bonding electrons)

question: Bond order normally gives idea of stability of a molecular species. All the molecules viz. ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}\,\,{\rm{L}}{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{2}}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{{\rm{B}}_{\rm{2}}}$  have the same bond order yet they are not equally stable. Their stability order is
explanation:
The molecular orbital configuration of the given molecules is ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = \sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ (no electron anti-bonding)
${\rm{L}}{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = \sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ (two anti-bonding electrons)
${{\rm{B}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = \sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,\left\{ {{\rm{\pi 2p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{1}}{\rm{ = \pi 2p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{1}}} \right\}$
(4 anti-bonding electrons)
Though the bond order of all the species are same (B.O = 1) but stability is different. This is due to difference in the presence of no. of anti bonding electron.
Higher the no. of anti-bonding electron lower is the stabiltiy hence the correct order is ${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ > L}}{{\rm{i}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{B}}_{\rm{2}}}$

question: Using MO theory, predict which of the following species has the shortest bond length?
explanation:
Bond order
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\left( \begin{array}{l}{\rm{No}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{bonding}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electrons - }}\\{\rm{No}}{\rm{.}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{of}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{anti - bonding}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{electrons}}\end{array} \right)}}{{\rm{2}}}$
Bond order in ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10 - 5}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 2}}{\rm{.5}}$
Bond order in ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10 - 7}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 1}}{\rm{.5}}$
Bond order in ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10 - 8}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 1}}$
Bond order in ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{10 - 4}}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ = 3}}$
Since Bond order $ \propto \frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{Bond}}\,\,{\rm{length}}}}$
$\therefore $ Bond length is shortest in ${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}$.

question: Which one of the following pairs of species have the same bond order?
explanation:

${\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}\left( {{\rm{8 + 8 + 1 = 17}}} \right){\rm{;C}}{{\rm{N}}^{\rm{ - }}}\left( {{\rm{6 + 7 + 1 = 14}}} \right)$
${\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}^{\rm{ + }}}\left( {{\rm{7 + 8 - 1 = 14}}} \right){\rm{;C}}{{\rm{N}}^{\rm{ + }}}\left( {{\rm{6 + 7 - 1 = 12}}} \right)$
We find ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{N}}^{\rm{ - }}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{O}}^{\rm{ + }}}$ both have 14 electrons so they have same bond order.

$\therefore {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Bond}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{order = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{4 - 2}}} \right){\rm{ = 1}}$
${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }} = \sigma 1{s^2}{\sigma ^ * }1{s^2}\sigma 2{s^1}$
${\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{.}}\,\,\,\,\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{3 - 2}}} \right){\rm{0}}{\rm{.5}}$
${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}{\rm{ = \sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{1}}}$
${\rm{B}}{\rm{.O}}{\rm{. = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}\left( {{\rm{4 - 3}}} \right){\rm{0}}{\rm{.5}}$
The bond order of ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$ and ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ is same but ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$ is more stable than ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ because ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$ is smaller in size and has 2 electrons in antibonding orbitals whereas ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$ has 3 electrons in antibonding orbitals. Hence ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ + }}$ is more stable than ${\rm{Li}}_{\rm{2}}^{\rm{ - }}$.

question: It is believed that atoms combine with each other such that the outermost shell acquires a stable configuration of 8 electrons.If stability were attained with 6 electrons rather than 8,what would be the formula of the stable flouride ion?

question: Which of the following has octet around central atom?
explanation: In a chemical bond, electrons are transferred or shared such that each atom attains an electronic configuration of a noble gas $\left(\mathrm{ns}^{2} \mathrm{np}\right.$ ) i.e. having 8 outer shell (valency) electron. This arrangement is in accordance with octet rule. An outermost shell which had eight electrons is called octet. Carbon tertrachloride has octet around central atom. The number of valence electrons in given compounds are as follows:$\mathrm{PF}_{5}\left(10 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right), \mathrm{SF}_{6}\left(12 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right), \mathrm{CCl}_{4}\left(8 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right), \mathrm{BF}_{3}\left(6 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Amongst the following, the total number of species not having eight electrons around central atom in its outer most shell, is
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}, \mathrm{BeCl}_{2}, \mathrm{CCl}_{4}, \mathrm{PCl}_{5}$
explanation:
Total number of species $=3$
$\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ : $1$ lone pair $+ 3$ bond pairs $=8 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}: 3$ bond pairs so, $6 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\mathrm{BeCl}_{2}: 2$ bond pairs so, $4 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\mathrm{CCl}_{4}: 4$ bond pairs so, $8 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5}: 5$ bond pairs so, $10 \mathrm{e}^{-}$
$\therefore \mathrm{AlCl}_{3}, \mathrm{BeCl}_{2}, \mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ do not possess $8 \mathrm{e}^{-}$around central atom.

question: According to Fajan's rules, which of the following is not correct about covalent character?
explanation: According to Fajan's rule, a smaller cation will have greater polarizing power and will impart greater covalent character. Thus, $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$  being smaller than $\mathrm{K}^{+}$, will result in LiF being more covalent than  ${\text{KF}}.$
subject: Chemistry

question: Highest covalent character is found in which of the following?
explanation:
"Greater is the polarisation, greater is the covalent character." For anions to get polarised, the size of an anion must be big in size, to get distorted by the cation, therefore, greater the covalent character. Among the given ionic compounds, the iodide anion has the largest size, it undergoes polarisation to larger extent.
Hence, $\mathrm{CaI}_{2}$ has the largest covalent character.
subject: Chemistry

question: The molecule/ion with square pyramidal shape is
explanation:

${\mathrm{\mathrm{PF}_{5}, \mathrm{PCl}_{5}-\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}}}$ hybridisation ${\mathrm{-5 \mathrm{bp}+}}$ no ${\mathrm{\mathrm{lp}-}}$ trigonal bipyramidal
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}-\mathrm{dsp}^{2}}}$ hybridisation $-$no ${\mathrm{\mathrm{lp}-}}$ square planar.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is least ionic?
explanation:
If electronegativity difference between two atoms is more then compound will be ionic in nature. Electronegativity values of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Ba}, \mathrm{Ag}}}$ and Cl are respectively ${\mathrm{1.88,0.82,0.89,1.93}}$ and 3.2.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{CoCl}_{2}=3.2-1.88=1.32}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{KCl}=3.2-0.82=2.38}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}=3.2-0.89=2.31}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}=3.2-1.93=1.27}}$
Hence, AgCl is least ionic compound.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Chemistry

question:
Arrange the bonds in order of increasing ionic character in the molecules :
${\mathrm{\mathrm{LiF}, \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{N}_{2}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{ClF}_{3}}}$.
explanation:
Greater the electronegativity difference between the atoms, greater will be the ionic character of molecule. So, increasing order of ionic character in the molecules is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}_{2} < \mathrm{SO}_{2} < \mathrm{ClF}_{3} < \mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{LiF}}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is the correct order of stability for the given superoxides
explanation: With progressive increase in the size of alkali metal ions,the stability of superoxides increases because the size of superoxide ion is large and large cation can be stabilised more by large anion.Therefore ,the order of stability is ${\rm{K}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}} < {\rm{CsO}} < {\rm{Rb}}{{\rm{O}}_2}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following compound is most covalent?
explanation:
The magnitude of covalent character depends upon a number of factors. These factors are:
(I) Small size of cation.
(II) Large size of anion.
(III) Large charge on either of the two ions.
Hence, due to larger size of $\mathrm{I}^{-}$, LiI is most covalent.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Order of covalent bond :
A. ${\rm{KF}} > {\rm{KI}}\,;{\rm{LiF}} > {\rm{KF}}$
B. ${\rm{KF}} < {\rm{KI}}\,;{\rm{LiF}} > {\rm{KF}}$
C. ${\rm{SnC}}{{\rm{l}}_4} > {\rm{SnC}}{{\rm{l}}_2};{\rm{CuCl}} > {\rm{NaCl}}$
D. ${\rm{LiF}} > {\rm{KF}}\,;{\rm{CuCl}} < {\rm{NaCl}}$
E. ${\rm{KF}} < {\rm{KI}}\,;{\rm{CuCl}} > {\rm{NaCl}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
Large cation, small anion, low charge favour ionic bond. Large anion, small cation ,higher charges favour covalent bond. Covalent character of:
$ \bullet $ ${\rm{KF}} < {\rm{KI}}$ since ${{\rm{I}}^ - }$ has larger size than ${{\rm{F}}^ - }$.
$ \bullet $ ${\rm{LiF}} > {\rm{KF}}$ since ${\rm{L}}{{\rm{i}}^ + }$ has smaller size than ${{\rm{K}}^ + }$
$ \bullet $ ${\rm{SnC}}{{\rm{l}}_4} > {\rm{SnC}}{{\rm{l}}_2}$ since ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{n}}^{4 + }}$ has more positive charge than ${\rm{S}}{{\rm{n}}^{2 + }}$.
$ \bullet $ ${\rm{CuCl}} > {\rm{NaCl}}$ since ${\rm{C}}{{\rm{u}}^ + }$ has smaller size than ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^ + }$.
So, the correct order of covalent bond is given in B, C and E only.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which halide of magnesium has highest ionic character?
explanation:
Magnesium fluoride $\left( {{\rm{Mg}}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right)$ has the highest ionic character. In ionic bonding, there is large electronegativity difference between two atoms, causing the bonding to be more polar have maximum ionic character.
${\rm{Mg}}{{\rm{I}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ < MgB}}{{\rm{r}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ < MgC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ < Mg}}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{.}}$

explanation: ${\rm{Ca}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$ has maximum covalent character due to large size of anion and possesses lowest lattice energy. Thus, melting point is lowest.

question: Which of the following metal halide is more covalent?

Higher the charge on the cation, greater is its polarising power. In ${\rm{SbC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{5}}}$, Antimony (Sb) is in +5 oxidation state. It has maximum polarising power. Hence, possess higher covalent character.

question: Identify the option in which the molecules are arranged in the correct order of their dipole moments
explanation: The correct order of dipole moment is $\mathrm{BF}_{3} < \mathrm{NF}_{3} < \mathrm{NH}_{3} . \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ have pyramidal geometry where central nitrogen has 3 bond pairs and 1 lone pair. Hence, both are $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$-hybridised. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, dipole vector of bond and lone pair are in same direction while in $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$, they are in opposite direction. Hence, dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is larger than $\mathrm{NF}_{3} . \mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is a symmetrical molecule where dipole vectors are arranged at $120^{\circ}$. Therefore, the resultant of two dipole vectors cancel out with the third dipole vector. Hence it's dipole moment will be zero.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which is the correct order of dipole moments of $\mathrm{BF}_{3}, \mathrm{NF}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ ?
explanation:
$\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ has zero dipole moment. In $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{~N}$ is more electronegative than H . So, nitrogen pulls the electron from H towards itself and the direction of moment is same due to $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}$ bond as that of the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957847/preptracker/questions/q_103977_explanation_1764957847360.png
In $\mathrm{NF}_{3}, \mathrm{~F}$ is more electronegative than N. All F atoms pull the electrons towards themselves and resultant dipole moment is opposite to the direction of that of the lone pair at N atom. Hence, net dipole moment decreases.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957848/preptracker/questions/q_103977_explanation_1764957848190.png
Therefore, dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is greater than $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$. Hence, order of dipole moment is$\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{NF}_{3}>\mathrm{BF}_{3}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark correct option given below:
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ has no dipole moment, whereas $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ have dipole moment.
$\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$ is ionic, whereas $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$ is covalent.
explanation: $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ is non-polar $(\mu=0)$ whereas $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ are polar $(\mu \neq 0)$. Hence, statement I is correct.  $\mathrm{SnCl}_{2}$ is ionic in aqueous medium whereas $\mathrm{SnCl}_{4}$ is covalent as lower oxidation favours ionic bond and higher oxidation state favours covalent bond. Hence statement II is correct.

options.text: Both Statement  A  and Statement B are not correct
Statement A is correct but Statement B is not correct
Both Statement A and Statement B are correct
Statement A is not correct but Statement B is correct

subject: Chemistry

question:
Identify the correct statements from the following.
(i) The dipole moment of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is zero
(ii) The dipole moment of $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ is higher than the dipole moment of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
(iii) The dipole moment of HI is lower than the dipole moment of HCl .
explanation:
i. The $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ molecule has a symmetrical trigonal planar geometry, the moment of any two $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{F}$ dipoles is equal in magnitude but opposite in direction to the moment of the $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ molecule is zero, same as $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$.
ii. In $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{~N}$ is more electronegative than H so, N pulls the electrons from H towards itself so, the direction of moment due to the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds is in the same direction as that of the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951330/preptracker/questions/q_103965_explanation_1764951330033.png
But in case of N - lone pair moment is in the opposite direction to the N-F bond moments. Thus, $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$ has smaller net dipole moment.
iii. Electronegativity of I-atom is less than of Cl -atom. Hence, HI has lower dipole-moment than HCl .
subject: Chemistry

Since fluorine is more electronegative than nitrogen, the net dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ is greater than ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$.
In ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ ,the orbital dipole due to lone pair and the dipole moment of NH bonds are in opposite direction,but in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ the orbital dipole due to lone pair and dipole moments of N-F bonds are in same direction.
explanation:

${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}=4.90 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}}}$
Resultant dipole moment in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}=0.80 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}}}$
In ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$, the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the N-H bonds, whereas in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$, the orbital dipole is in the direction opposite to the resultant dipole moment of the three N-F bonds.
So, the correct option is (4).

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct options given below:

Assertion: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ molecules have pyramidal shape with a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom. The resultant dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ is greater than that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$.

Reason: In ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ ,the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the N-H bonds.F is the most electronegative element.
explanation:
Both the molecules ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ have pyramidal shape with a lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom.
Although fluorine is more electronegative than nitrogen, the resultant dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}\left(4.90 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}\right)}}$ is greater than that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}\left(0.8 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{Cm}\right)}}$. This is because, in case of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{H}}}$ bonds whereas in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ the orbital dipole is in the direction opposite to the resultant dipole moment of the three N-F bonds. The orbital dipole because of lone pair decreases the effect of the resultant bond moment which results in low dipole moment of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NF}_{3}}}$ as represented below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959067/preptracker/questions/q_17381_explanation_1764959066614.png So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry

question: group moment is $2.3 \,\mathrm{D}$ and $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group moment is $0.4 \,\mathrm{D}$. Determine the dipole moment of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$ in $\mathrm{cm} \times 10^{30}$ in nearest possible integer.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959057/preptracker/questions/q_17274_explanation_1764959056904.png
The resultant dipole moment of two $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}$ bonds.
$=\sqrt{(0.4)^{2}+(0.4)^{2}+2 \times(0.4)^{2} \times \cos 120^{\circ}}$
$ = \sqrt {0.32 - 0.16} = 0.4\,\,{\rm{D}}$
$\therefore$ Net dipole moment of the molecule $ = 2.3 + 0.4 = 2.7\,\,{\rm{D}}$
$1 \mathrm{D}=10^{-18}$ esu $-\mathrm{cm} .=10^{-20}$ esu-m.
$4.8 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,\,{\rm{esu}} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}{\rm{C}}$
$\therefore 1$ esu $=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$.
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{D}=\dfrac{1}{3} \times 10^{-29} \mathrm{cm}=\dfrac{10}{3} \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}$.
$\therefore$ Net dipole moment of $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$
$=2.7 \times \dfrac{10}{3} \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}=9 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{cm}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assertion: According to Fajan's rule, covalent character is favoured by small cation and large anion.

Reason: The magnitude of covalent character in the ionic bond depends upon the extent of polarisation.
explanation: Fajan's rules states that a smaller cation and a larger anion, along with a higher charge on the cation, increase the covalent character of an ionic bond by enhancing polarisation, resulting in greater electron charge buildup between nuclei and a higher percentage of covalent character. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{NaCl}$ is more ionic than $\mathrm{LiCl}$.

Reason: Greater the size of cation, greater is the polarising power.
explanation: In the case of $\mathrm{LiCl}$, the smaller $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$cation can polarize the electron cloud of the larger $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$anion more effectively, leading to a stronger partial covalent character in the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond. On the other hand, in $\mathrm{NaCl}$, the larger size of the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$cation results in less effective polarization, and the ionic bond is primarily ionic in nature.  So the option (3) is correct.

question: A diatomic molecule has a dipole moment of 1.2 D. If its bond distance is ${\rm{1}}{\rm{.0}}{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{A^\circ }}$, what fraction of an electronic charge exists on each atom?
explanation:
For a diatomic molecule dipole moment ${\rm{\mu = e \times d}}$
$\therefore $ electronic charge ${\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{\mu }}}{{\rm{d}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 18}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}\,\,{\rm{cm}}}}{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.0 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 8}}}}\,\,{\rm{cm}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\,\,{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}$
Actual value of electronic charge
${\rm{ = 4}}{\rm{.8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}\,\,{\rm{esu}}{\rm{.}}$
$\therefore $ Fraction of the electronic charge
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{1}}{\rm{.2 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}}}{{{\rm{4}}{\rm{.8 \times 1}}{{\rm{0}}^{{\rm{ - 10}}}}}}{\rm{ = 0}}{\rm{.25}}{\rm{.}}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which one of the following compounds is having maximum 'lone pair-lone pair' electron repulsions?
explanation:
More the number of lone pair of electrons in molecule, greater will be the lone pair-lone pair repulsion.
1. $\mathrm{ClF}_{3}$ - Central atom Cl has 3 bond pairs and 2 lone pairs.
2. $\mathrm{IF}_{5}$ - I has 5 bond pairs and 1 lone pair
3. $\mathrm{SF}_{4}-\mathrm{S}$ has 4 bond pairs and 1 lone pair
4. $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}-\mathrm{Xe}$ has 2 bond pairs and 3 lone pairs.
Since, lone pairs are maximum in $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$. Hence, it has maximum lone pair-lone pair repulsion.

explanation: In octahedral molecule ${\rm{M}}{{\rm{X}}_{\rm{6}}}$, the six hybrid orbitals $\left(\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}\right)$ are directed towards the corners of a regular octahedral with an angle of $90^{\circ}$ According to given structure of ${\rm{M}}{{\rm{X}}_{\rm{6}}}$, the number of ${\rm{X}} - {\rm{M}} - {\rm{X}}$ bonds at $180^\circ $must be three. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953210/preptracker/questions/q_17017_explanation_1764953209822.png

question: Among the following, the pair in which the two species are not isostructural, is
explanation:
$\mathrm{SiF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{SF}_{4}$ are not isostructural because${\rm{Si}}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{4}}}$ is tetrahedral due to $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$-hybridisation of $\mathrm{Si}$.
${ }_{14} \mathrm{Si}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 3 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{p}^{2}$ (in ground state)
${ }_{14} \mathrm{Si}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{6}, 3 \mathrm{~s}^{1} 3 \mathrm{p}^{3} \quad \text { (in excited state) }
$Hence, four equivalent $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$-hybrid orbitals are obtained and they are overlapped by four $\mathrm{p}$-orbitals of four fluorine atoms on their axis. Thus, it shows following structures. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959022/preptracker/questions/q_17013_explanation_1764959022266.png While $\mathrm{SF}_{4}$ is not tetrahedral but it is distorted tetrahedral because in it, $\mathrm{S}$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}$ hybridised.
$_{{\rm{16}}}{\rm{\,S = 1\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,2\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}{\rm{,3\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{1}}$
(in ground state)
${\rm{ = 1\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,2\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}{\rm{,3\,}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{3p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{1}}\,{\rm{3\,d}}_{{\rm{xy}}}^{\rm{1}}$
$\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}$-hybridisation
(in first excitation state) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764959023/preptracker/questions/q_17013_explanation_1764959023031.png Hence, five $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}$ hybrid orbitals are obtained. One orbital is already paired and rest four are overlapped with four $\mathrm{p}$-orbitals of four fluorine atoms on their axis in trigonal bipyramidal form. This structure is distorted from trigonal bipyramidal to see-saw due to involvement of repulsion between lone pair and bond pair.
subject: Chemistry

question:
${\rm{S}}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{4}}}$ molecule has two type of structure as shown in below figure, in which structure (b) is more stable than (a).
lp-lp repulsions decide the greater stability of sturucture (b).
explanation:
In (a), the lone pair is present at axial position, so there are there lp-lp repulsions at ${\rm{90^\circ }}$. In (b) the lone pair is at equatorial position and there are two lp-lp repulsions. Hence, arrangement (b) is more stable. The shape shown in (b) is describedas a distrorted tetrahedron, a folded square or a see-saw.
So, the option (3) is correct.

question:
Lone pair-lone pair repulsive interactions are greater than lone pair-bond pair and bond pair-bond pair interactions.
The space occupied by lone pair electrons is more as compared to bond pair electrons.
explanation:
While the lone pairs are localised on the central atom, each bonded pair is shared between two atoms. As a result, the lone pair electrons in a molecule occupy more space as compared to the bonding pairs of electrons. This results in greater repulsion between lone pairs of electrons as compared to the lone pair-bond pair, bond pair and bond pair - repulsions.
So, the option (3) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct

question:
Assertion: Sigma $(\sigma)$ is a strong bond, while pi $(\pi)$ is a weak bond.

Reason: Atoms rotate freely about pi $(\pi)$ bond.
explanation: Sigma bonds result from head-on overlap, with electron density along the bond axis. This allows free rotation, making them stronger. Pi bonds, involving lateral overlap, particularly in $\mathrm{p}$ orbitals, are weaker due to less effective overlap. This geometry restricts rotation around the bond axis. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Consider the following statements
I. In ${{\rm{F}}_{\rm{2}}}$ molecule, ${\rm{F}}\, - \,{\rm{F}}$ ${\rm{\sigma }}\, - $ bond is formed by axial overlapping of two p-orbitals.
II. In ${{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$ molecule, one ${\rm{\sigma }}\, - $ bond is formed by axial overlap of p-orbitals and one ${\rm{\pi }}\, - $ bond is formed by lateral overlap of p-orbitals.
III. In ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ molecule, one ${\rm{\sigma }}$ and two ${\rm{\pi }}\, - $ bonds, i.e., all the three bonds are formed by lateral overlap of p-orbitals.
Which of the above statements is/are incorrect?
explanation:
Statement III is incorrect
It’s correct form is as follows:
In ${{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}$ molecule, one ${\rm{\sigma }}\, - $ bond is formed by axial overlapping of p - orbitals and two ${\rm{\pi }}\, - $ bonds are formed by lateral overlapping of p - orbitals.
So, the option (3) is correct.
